food_name: Greek Yoghurt Bowl (Full-Fat Plain Greek Yoghurt, Granola, Mixed Berries, Honey)
region_of_origin: Singapore (popularised globally from Greek strained yoghurt tradition; adopted in Singapore health cafés from approximately 2013)
food_subgroup: Full-fat plain Greek yoghurt topped with granola, fresh mixed berries, and honey — a high-protein, moderate-GI breakfast bowl

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has entries for yoghurt (FOODON:03301776) but no specific entry for Greek yoghurt bowl with granola and berries. Closest parent is yoghurt-based breakfast bowl. No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Yoghurt, plain — closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for the dominant component (full-fat plain Greek yoghurt). Composite dish not individually coded in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for Greek yoghurt bowl with granola and berries. A00LC (yoghurt) used as closest parent anchor for the dominant ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Greek yoghurt bowl composite dish. SNOMED CT food ontology does not extend to composite breakfast bowls at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl (345g) consisting of approximately 200g full-fat plain Greek yoghurt, 30g granola, 100g mixed fresh berries (blueberry, strawberry, raspberry), and 10g honey with 5g garnish, as served at Singapore health cafés.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — component analysis: plain whole-milk Greek yoghurt (FDC ID 170903), granola (FDC ID 169761), mixed berries (blueberry FDC ID 171711, strawberry FDC ID 167762, raspberry FDC ID 167755), honey (FDC ID 169640)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Composite dish nutrient values derived by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for each ingredient at the stated portion weights (200g yoghurt + 30g granola + 100g berries + 10g honey + 5g garnish = 345g total), then normalised to per-100g values. No single USDA entry exists for this composite dish. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not carry a Greek yoghurt bowl entry; USDA SR Legacy used as the highest-quality approved alternative.

gi_source.title: Composite GI estimated from component GI values: full-fat plain Greek yoghurt GI ~11 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); granola GI ~55–65 (University of Sydney GI Database); blueberries GI ~53, strawberries GI ~40, raspberries GI ~32 (University of Sydney GI Database); honey GI ~55 (University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted composite GI ~52 at stated portion weights.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No single GI measurement exists for this composite Greek yoghurt bowl. Composite GI calculated using weighted average of component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Portion weights: 200g yoghurt (GI 11), 30g granola (GI 60), 100g mixed berries (weighted GI ~44), 10g honey (GI 55). Glycemic load per 345g serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (52 × 53.5g) / 100 ≈ 27.8g. GI 52 is classified as low (GI ≤ 55) per the University of Sydney GI Database classification system used by Atkinson et al. 2008, which defines low GI as ≤ 55, medium as 56–69, and high as ≥ 70.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (40mg/100g), high calcium (132mg/100g), ACE-inhibitory peptides from fermentation, and potassium from berries support blood pressure management via DASH-aligned mechanisms.
hypertension.guidance: This bowl is beneficial for hypertension: sodium is very low (138mg per 345g serving), calcium content (~455mg per bowl, ~45% of daily RDA) supports blood pressure via the DASH mechanism, and fermentation-derived ACE-inhibitory peptides in Greek yoghurt have demonstrated mild antihypertensive effects in clinical studies; patients on antihypertensive medication should maintain consistent potassium intake from the berry component.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Composite GI ~52 (low) and moderate glycemic load (~27.8 per bowl); full-fat Greek yoghurt component has very low GI (~11) and high protein (8g/100g) supporting satiety and glycaemic control; granola and honey are the primary glycaemic drivers.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Suitable for Type 2 Diabetes with portion awareness: the full-fat Greek yoghurt base (GI ~11) and berry components (GI 32–53) are T2DM-friendly, but granola (GI ~60) and honey (GI ~55) raise the composite GL to ~27.8 per bowl — patients should consider reducing granola to 15g and omitting honey to lower GL to approximately 18–21, and the probiotic cultures (L. bulgaricus, S. thermophilus) have emerging evidence for improved insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is moderate (4.5g/100g, ~15.5g per bowl) from full-fat dairy; however, full-fat dairy saturated fat has neutral-to-beneficial cardiovascular outcomes in current evidence; trans fat is zero; berries provide anthocyanins with anti-inflammatory and endothelial-protective effects.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Suitable for cardiovascular disease management: trans fat is zero, the saturated fat (4.5g/100g) comes from full-fat dairy which current evidence (including PURE study and meta-analyses) associates with neutral-to-beneficial cardiovascular outcomes compared to refined carbohydrates; berry anthocyanins provide anti-inflammatory and endothelial-protective benefits; patients with established CVD should monitor total caloric intake given the moderate energy density (148 kcal/100g, ~510 kcal per bowl).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Probiotic cultures (L. bulgaricus, S. thermophilus) support gut microbiome modulation via the gut-joint axis; calcium and vitamin D support bone protection in RA patients at elevated osteoporosis risk; berry anthocyanins provide anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis: probiotic cultures in Greek yoghurt modulate gut microbiome diversity and reduce intestinal permeability (gut-joint axis), calcium (~455mg per bowl) and vitamin D (~2.8mcg per bowl) support bone health in RA patients at elevated osteoporosis risk from chronic inflammation and corticosteroid use, and berry anthocyanins provide clinically relevant anti-inflammatory activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Greek Yoghurt Bowl (希腊酸奶碗) is a popular health café breakfast in Singapore, consisting of thick, creamy full-fat plain Greek yoghurt topped with crunchy granola, fresh mixed berries (blueberry, strawberry, raspberry), and a drizzle of honey, served in a single bowl of approximately 345g. Greek yoghurt is strained yoghurt — regular yoghurt drained of its whey liquid — resulting in a much thicker, higher-protein, lower-carbohydrate product than regular yoghurt. Full-fat Greek yoghurt (~10% fat, ~4.1g carbohydrate per 100g) has significant clinical advantages over low-fat versions: lower carbohydrate content, higher satiety, and emerging evidence for better Type 2 Diabetes outcomes with full-fat versus low-fat dairy. The live probiotic cultures (Lactobacillus bulgaricus, Streptococcus thermophilus) contribute to gut microbiome diversity, which is clinically relevant for rheumatoid arthritis patients via the gut-joint axis. Nutritionally, the bowl provides 8g protein per 100g (27.6g per serving), 7.5g fat per 100g, 15.5g carbohydrate per 100g, and 148 kcal per 100g (~510 kcal per 345g bowl). The composite GI is approximately 52 (low by University of Sydney classification, where low GI is defined as ≤ 55), with a glycemic load of ~27.8 per bowl. Berries are among the most T2DM-friendly fruits (GI 32–53) and provide vitamin C (12mg/100g) and anthocyanins with anti-inflammatory properties. Calcium content is high (132mg/100g, ~455mg per bowl — approximately 45% of daily RDA), supporting blood pressure via the DASH mechanism. Sodium is very low (40mg/100g). The granola is the most calorie-dense and highest-GI component; reducing granola to 15g and omitting honey lowers the GL to approximately 18–21, making the bowl more suitable for T2DM management. Available at Singapore health cafés including Grain, Hmlet Kitchen, and PS.Café, and easily prepared at home.
common_user_queries: is Greek yoghurt bowl good for blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component analysis for each named ingredient at stated portion weights, normalised to per-100g. USDA SR Legacy is an approved primary source. GI values sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), both approved GI sources. Composite dish not available as a single entry in any approved database; component-level analysis is the appropriate and documented methodology. All values are internally consistent and plausible for this food type.
notes: 1. Composite dish: no single approved-source entry exists for this exact Greek yoghurt bowl formulation. Nutrient values derived by weighted component analysis (200g full-fat plain Greek yoghurt + 30g granola + 100g mixed berries + 10g honey + 5g garnish = 345g), normalised to per-100g, using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for each component. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not carry a Greek yoghurt bowl entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; individual component fatty acid data would require further disaggregation beyond the scope of this record. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a composite entry. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for all components per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data. 5. GI category is 'low': GI 52 falls in the low range (≤ 55) per the University of Sydney GI Database classification system (low ≤ 55, medium 56–69, high ≥ 70) as used in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The previous iteration incorrectly applied a different classification boundary (51–69 for medium). The University of Sydney standard — the approved GI source — defines low as ≤ 55, placing GI 52 firmly in the low category. 6. FoodEx2 code A00LC (yoghurt) used as closest parent anchor; no composite FoodEx2 code exists for this dish. 7. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low'. GI 52 is classified as low (≤ 55) per the University of Sydney GI Database classification system — the approved authoritative GI source. Iteration 3: Updated notes item 5 to document the GI classification correction and cite the University of Sydney standard.
